outcomes: Understanding the fundamentals of programming, from its core concepts and its importance in real world implementation.

Interacting with the Pictoblox application to understand its fundamentals, including its interface, basic features, and how it can be used as a visual programming tool to introduce learners to coding concepts.
Making your first piece simple and fun piece of code to better understand programming concepts and fundamentals of Pictoblox.
introduction: In this lesson, you will learn the fundamentals of programming, including its core concepts and importance. You will interact with the Pictoblox interface, a user-friendly platform for coding. By the end of the session, you will create a simple yet fun piece of code, allowing you to apply what you've learned and experience the joy of bringing your ideas to life through programming. This hands-on approach will build your confidence and spark your creativity.

﻿

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content:
In this session, you will get an introduction to the fundamentals of programming and the PictoBlox software.

First, you'll learn what a program is by exploring examples from everyday life, such as games and traffic lights. You'll also learn that programming involves planning, writing, and debugging code, and that the order of instructions is crucial for a program to work correctly.

Next, you will be introduced to the PictoBlox interface and its main components. You'll discover the purpose of the Stage, Sprites (like Tobi the bear), and Blocks, which are the building blocks of your programs. You'll also learn that a script is a program you create by arranging these blocks in the scripting area.

Finally, you will apply these concepts by building your very first program. You'll create a script to make Tobi the bear walk across the screen, learning how to use essential blocks from the Events, Control, and Motion palettes to control his movement, rotation style, and speed. You'll also learn how to save your completed project.

mainConcepts.title: STEPS
mainConcepts.content:
What is a Program?

A program is a set of instructions that a computer follows to complete a task. The computer receives the information, handles it, utilizes it, and then gives an output based on it by following a program.

We are surrounded by computers and computer programs; phones or tablets you can play or watch videos on. Other example are traffic lights and they follow different programs to carry out different tasks.

Let's See How Programs Work!

When you want to play your favorite game, like "Minecraft" or "Roblox," you tap its picture, right? A special program inside the tablet quickly makes the game start for you! It's like the tablet knows exactly how to open the game because of those instructions.

Another example is that when you're in a car, you see traffic lights turn red, then yellow, then green. They don't just change randomly! A program inside the traffic light tells it exactly when to turn red (so cars stop) and when to turn green (so cars go).

According to Linda Liukas, a Finnish computer programmer, children’s writer, and programming instructor, programming consists of three parts:Planning the programWriting the program in a programming language into a codeTesting and debugging the program

The second part is what everybody generally considers programming. Only this part requires coding skills; the first and third parts require additional skills like problem-solving, and decomposition (the ability to break down bigger problems into smaller, manageable tasks).

Programming AnalogyGetting Dressed

On days that you have to go to school, your mother and father are behind you in the morning, asking you to wake up, while you keep saying ‘5 more minutes!’ until you realize it is late and you don’t have time to take bath. So, you decide to just wash your face, brush your teeth, and get dressed. To get dressed, you follow a particular sequence of steps, like the one given below:

Take off the pajamas

Take off the undergarments from the previous day

Wear a fresh pair of undergarments

Put on the school uniformWear accessories such as the belt, tie, and ID cardWear socksWear shoes

Here, as you can see, you followed a set of instructions to complete a task, which is getting dressed (though hastily, and without taking a bath!).

Now, you know that to properly get dressed, you need to follow the order; you won’t wear a fresh pair of undergarments before taking off the previous ones, nor would you put on the uniform first and then wear undergarments (unless you’re Clark Kent! 🕴🏼).

Similarly, you must write the program in a particular order so that the computer does the task given to it correctly, and we get the result that we expect.

PictoBlox – Your Perfect Programming Partner

PictoBlox is a graphical programming software based on Scratch blocks and is the ideal companion for setting the first step into the world of programming.

PictoBlox Interface

Now that you have PictoBlox installed on your desktop, let’s take you on a tour around it!

Stage

The stage is a background or a backdrop for your Scratch projects (the programs you’ll create). It is a white background in the top left corner; you will see a bear standing there. His name is Tobi and he is what is called a sprite (we’ll see in a moment what it is). The Stage is where the sprite moves, draws, and interacts with other sprites and/or hardware. It has its own set of scripts, images, and sounds.

Sprite

A Sprite is an object or a character that performs different actions in the projects. It understands and obeys the instructions that you’ll give them in your projects. It can move and can be moved to any place on the stage (you can try by clicking on them and moving them around).

Blocks

Block is like jigsaw puzzle pieces that fit into each other. They are predefined commands used to create programs by simply dragging and dropping them below one another in the scripting area.

ScriptA script is a program or a code in PictoBlox/Scratch lingo. It is a set of ‘blocks’ that are arranged below one another in a specific order to perform a task or a series of tasks.The scripting area is where you will create your scripts.

Activity: Make Tobi WalkReady for some action? Let’s make a small script to make Tobi walk.

Let’s Code

Follow the steps below:Create a New file in PictoBlox:Follow the step if you are working on Windows, macOS, or Linux:Open PictoBlox and create a new file.

Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

Follow the step if you are working on Android or iPhone:

Open PictoBlox App and select My Space Tab from the home screen.

Click on + sign to create a new file.

The new file should be created like this.

Open the Events palette and drag and drop the when flag clicked block into the scripting area.

Then when the flag-clicked block is a special type of block called a hat block. It is used to start a script.

Using this particular hat block you can run the script by clicking on the green flag above the stage.

Next, open the Control palette and drag and drop the forever block below the when flag clicked block.

This block is used when you want a set of blocks to keep running until the script is stopped manually.

Then, open the Motion palette and drag and drop the move () steps inside the forever block.

This block is used to move the sprite by a specified number of steps. In our case, it is 10.

 

Run the script by clicking the green flag.

What just happened? Where did Tobi go?! As it seems, he went out of the frame.

Let’s fix this so that the Tobi doesn’t wander off again.

Open the Motion palette and drag and drop the if on edge, bounce block inside the forever block.

This block detects whether the sprite has touched the edge of the stage or not and changes the direction if it has.

Run the script by clicking the green flag.

What is happening to Tobi Now? Is he upside-down? To stop this, you must change the rotation style.

Go to the Motion palette and drag and drop the set rotation style () below the when flag clicked block and select left-right from the drop-down.

Run the script by clicking the green flag.

Isn’t Tobi moving very fast? Let’s slow him down.

Open the Control palette and drag and drop the wait () seconds block below the move () steps to block and change the delay value to 0.1 seconds.

The wait block is used to pause/delay the script execution by the specified time.The script is now complete!

Run it by clicking the green flag.

Yay! You’ve just completed your first script! Pat yourself on the back.

Saving the Program

Follow the steps to save the file in Windows, macOS, and Linux:

To save the program, click on File > Save As.

A window will pop up. Choose the location as Desktop or any other relevant folder. Name the file Tobi Walking.

Click on Save. The file is saved with a .sb3 extension on your computer.

Follow the steps to save the file on Android and iPhone:

Click on the Save button.

A popup would open. Write the name of the project and click on Save.

You can find your saved file in My Space.

outcomes: Understanding how Sprites move in PictoBlox and how to control this movement using the Motion blocks for sprites in the stage environment.
Understanding the Block Palette as where all the colored puzzles pieces (coding blocks) are kept in PictoBlox.
Identifying the X and Y grid system used in PictoBlox and understanding how positive and negative numbers determine the position of a sprite.
Using PictoBlox to apply problem solving skills by identifying challenges, testing ideas, and improving their projects.
Using PictoBlox to strengthen logical thinking by analyzing information and making decisions while creating programs.
Practicing sequencing in programming by arranging coding blocks in the correct order to make their programs work properly.
introduction: In this project, you'll learn how to make things move on the screen using different programming blocks. You'll also understand how to use the X and Y grid to position things, like Tobi the character, in specific places on the stage. This will help you practice problem-solving, think logically about how to organize steps, and follow the right order (sequencing) when writing your code.

  • Each child adds one sentence to continue the story, going around the circle.

  • Encourage creativity and imagination, and see where the story goes.

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content: Drawing a sqaure

mainConcepts.title: Drawing a sqaure
mainConcepts.content:
Another cool thing that you can do in PictoBlox other than creating animations is drawing shapes.

Let explore it in this lesson.

What are Shapes?

A shape is an external form, boundary, or outline of an object. Some of the common shapes are:

  • Circle: is a closed two-dimensional figure in which the set of all the points in the plane is equidistant from a given point called “centre”.

  • Rectangle: s a closed 2-D shape, having 4 sides, 4 corners, and 4 right angles (90°). The opposite sides of a rectangle are equal and parallel.

  • Square: is a two-dimensional plane figure with four equal sides and all the four angles are equal to 90 degrees.

  • Triangle: is a three-sided polygon that consists of three edges and three vertices

  • Pentagon: is a geometrical shape, which has five sides and five angles. Here, “Penta” denotes five and “gon” denotes angle.

Backdrops: 

A backdrop is one of the many frames, or backgrounds, that a Stage can have. The Stage can change its look to any of its backdrops.

You can choose a backdrop from

  • The backdrop library

  • Uploading a file from the computer

  • Creating one using the paint editor

Costumes: 

A costume is one out of possibly many “frames” or alternate appearances of a sprite. Sprites can change their look to any of its costumes. Every sprite has at least one costume.

The costumes available for the sprite are shown in the Costume tab, next to the Code tab in the extreme left. There are four ways of getting a costume for a sprite or stage.

  • From the costume library

  • Drawing one yourself using the inbuilt paint editor

  • Getting an image or multiple images from your desktop

  • Taking an image using a webcam

Let’s Code

Follow the steps below:

  1. Create a New file in PictoBlox:

  3. Follow the step if you are working on Windows, macOS, or Linux:

  5. Open PictoBlox and create a new file.

  7. Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

  • First, we will set the Backdrop. Choose the Xy-grid backdrop from the Backdrop library.

  • Just like you need a pen (or a pencil) to draw on paper, you also need a pen to draw on the Stage. Therefore, let’s add a Pencil sprite from the sprite library.

  • In the sprite palette, you’ll see both Tobi and the pencil. Select Tobi and right-click on him. In doing so, a menu will drop down.

  • Click on ‘delete’.

Pen Extension

To draw anything with the pen, we will need pen extension blocks.

Click on the Add Extension button.

Then, click on the Pen extension to add use the blocks.

The Pen palette will appear.

Pen Extension Blocks

We will be using the following blocks from the pen extension in the next topic.

  1. When you run the pen down the block, you enable a mode where, whenever the sprite moves, it will draw lines on the stage.

  • When you run the pen up block, you enable a mode where the sprite does not draw while moving.

  • To erase all the lines, we will use erase all blocks.

Now that you know the basics, let’s draw some real shapes in the next topic.

Making the Square Manually

To make a square, follow the steps below:

  1. Click the pen down the block.

  2. Then, click the move 100 steps block.

  3. Next, click the turn clockwise 90 degrees block.

  4. Repeat steps 2 & 3 three more times.

 

 

Editing the costume

 

  2. Click the Costumes tab, you’ll see the pencil in the costume area on the left.

  4. Select the full costume by clicking on one end of the editor and dragging the mouse over and across the costume. The pencil will get highlighted. Now, we can move the pencil around.

  6. Click on the pencil and drag it in such a way that the tip of the pencil overlaps the center dot on the editor. After aligning them release the pencil.

Once you do it, you’ll observe that the line now is at the tip of the pencil on the stage. Success!

Making the Square using Script

  1. Now, let’s create the script to draw the square by stacking all the blocks together.

  3. To draw the square, you had to click each of the 3 blocks 4 times. To make a script that does the same, you need to add all 3 blocks in the given sequence 4 times!

  • Now when you click on the modified script, you’ll be able to see the pencil moving and drawing sides of the square one by one.

  • You can simplify all this by using a repeat block. Just add it around the 3 blocks and write 4 in the space.

  • Place a pen down block above the repeat block to draw the square and place a when () key pressed block above the entire script. That’s it.

And your square is ready!

Save the project file as Drawing Square.

outcomes: Understanding what a condition is by creating programs that make decisions based on specific rules or events.
Understand what a Loop is in Pictoblox.
Know the types of Conditions in Pictoblox.
Know the types of Loops in Pictoblox.
introduction: In this lesson, you will dive into the fascinating world of conditions in programming, an essential concept that allows computers to make decisions. You’ll learn how to create conditional programs using blocks like if, if-else, and loops, enabling your code to respond dynamically to different situations. Through engaging activities, you’ll use PictoBlox to make Tobi, your sprite, glide to random positions or follow your mouse pointer based on specified conditions. By the end of this session, you’ll have a solid understanding of conditional programming and how it enhances interactivity in your projects.

notes.title: Ice Breaker
notes.content:
Let’s kick things off with a fun activity called “True or False?” 

1. I’ll share a few statements with logical conditions, and you’ll decide if they’re TRUE or FALSE. For example: 

  - If it’s raining AND I have an umbrella, I’ll go outside. 

  - If 5 > 3 OR 2 > 4, the condition is true. 

  - NOT (5 < 3) is true. 

2. You can also share your own creative logic statements with the group!

notes.title: Activity
notes.content: Working with Conditions - In this activity, you will create instructions using conditions and also learn about where it can be used. You are going to use if and if else block to make Tobi perform actions based on if the condition is true or false.

notes.title: Wrap Activity
notes.content:

  • Ask participants to share their main takeaways or insights.

  • Provide additional resources, such as recommended books, websites, or online tutorials, for participants to explore further.

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content:
In our previous sessions, you learned the basics of programming with PictoBlox and enhanced your projects by creating various projects.

Today, we'll build on that foundation by diving into one of the most powerful concepts in programming: conditional logic. You'll discover how to make your programs smart enough to make decisions, just like you do every day.

By learning to use if/then and if/then/else blocks, you'll be able to create programs that respond to user actions and other specific conditions, making your projects more interactive .

mainConcepts.title: Working with conditions
mainConcepts.content:
• Life is full of conditions:

  • if you complete your homework, your mum will let me play, if you don’t, she will scold you.

  • if you study, you will pass your exams, but if you don't, you will fail your exams.

  • In the real world, you luckily have your brain to check what the condition is, whether it is met or not, and respond accordingly.

  • But a computer isn’t that lucky, unfortunately (or fortunately?🤷🏼‍♀️).

  • Even though it can perform every task at lightning-fast speed, you still need to give instructions to perform those tasks; even checking the conditions.

  • This is where the conditional statements come in handy. These statements allow the program to check the conditions by testing a variable against a value and act accordingly.

  • A program that has conditional statements is called a Conditional Program, and the process is known as Conditional Programming.

  • Conditional Blocks

  • Conditions are expressions that evaluate to either true or false. They are used to define the criteria upon which certain actions or decisions within a program depend. Conditions often involve the comparison of variables, values, or the result of other logical operations.Conditional statements, also known as control structures or flow control statements, are programming constructs that dictate the execution of different code blocks based on the evaluation of specified conditions

  • The if () then block will check whether the specified condition is true or not. If it is true, the blocks inside it will run, and then the script involved will continue.

  • If the condition is false, the code inside the block will be ignored and the script will move on. The condition is checked only once.

 

  • if () then else 

  • This block will check whether the specified condition is true or not. If the condition is true, the code held inside the first C (below the if arm) will run.

  • Then, the script will continue; if the condition is false, the code inside the second C (below the else arm) will run. (Unlike the if () then block).

  • The block wait() seconds pauses its script for the specified amount of seconds — the wait can also be a decimal number.

  • Activity

  • Let’s make a script to see it in action. We’re going to make a script that will make Tobi glide to a random position on the Stage whenever we press the space key.

  • Let’s Code

  • Follow the steps below:

  • Create a New file in PictoBlox:

  • Follow the step if you are working on Windows, macOS, or Linux:

  • Open PictoBlox and create a new file.

  • Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

  • Follow the steps below to make the script

  • Add an if () then block.

  • Place a key () pressed? block in the space of the if block.

  • Place glide () secs to () blocks under the if arm.

  • Place a forever block around the if () then block.

  • Add a when flag clicked block above to complete the script.

    Click the flag when done and press the space key.

  • if else condition mins

  • The if () then else block will check whether the specified condition is true or not.

  • If the condition is true, the code held inside the first C (below the if arm) will run.

  • Then, the script will continue; if the condition is false, the code inside the second C (below the else arm) will run. (unlike the if () then block).

  • Activity

  • Let’s make a script to see it in action.

  • We’re going to make a script that will make Tobi move towards the mouse cursor whenever we click it. When we’re not clicking it, he should go back to his default position in the center.

  • Let’s Code

  • Follow the steps below to make the script:

  • Add an if-else block and place a mouse-down? block in the space of the if arm.

  • If it is true, Tobi should glide towards the mouse.

  • Therefore, add a glide () secs to x: () y: () block and place mouse x and mouse y in their respective white spaces.

  • If the condition is false, it will stay at the center.

  • Thus, place another glide () secs to x: () y: () block and write set x and y value to 0.

  • Add a forever block to run the script continuously.

  • Add a when flag clicked block to run the script.

  • Click the flag when done and press the mouse.

  • ﻿Save the project file as Working with Conditions.

introduction:
In the last lesson, you learned how to make a sprite move on the stage. In this session, you will learn how to create a project where a sprite will follow another sprite all around the stage wherever it moves.

Let us make a game where the Beetle will follow the Taco and the Taco will follow the mouse pointer controlled by us. We will learn how to turn the direction of the sprite to make it face a certain position or object. Ready to make the Beetle run throughout the screen?

notes.title: Activity

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content:
In the last lesson, you learned how to make a sprite move on the stage. Now, let us create a project where a sprite will follow another sprite all around the stage wherever it moves.

Let's Get started!

mainConcepts.title: Beetle and Taco
mainConcepts.content:
Let’s Code!

  1. Open PictoBlox and create a new file. Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

  3. First, we will change the background of our stage, from a white background to blue.

  5. Click on Choose a Backdrop (bottom right corner).

  • Select the Blue Sky 2 backdrop from the library.

  • Click the Choose a Sprite button (bottom right corner) and add the sprites Beetle and Taco one by one from the library.

  • Delete the Tobi sprite by right clicking on it and click on delete.

  • Select the Taco sprite. Drag and drop the when flag clicked block from Events palette.

  • Now let’s reduce the size of the Taco using the block set size to () from Looks palette and set the size to 50%. Add the block go to () from Motion palette and select mouse-pointer from the drop-down to make the Taco follow the mouse.

  • To make the Taco follow the mouse continuously add a forever block from the Control palette for the above block.

  • Click on the flag and check if the Taco is following your mouse-pointer.

  • Now, let’s make the script for the Beetle. Select the Beetle sprite.

  • Drag and drop when flag clicked block from Events palette.

  • To make the Beetle appear in the front layer when it is moving on the Taco get the block go to () layer from the Looks palette and front is the default option selected.

  • Set the size of the Beetle to 50% using the block set size to () from Looks palette.

  • Now add the block point towards () from Motion palette and select Taco from the drop-down list to make it move in the same direction as Taco.

  • Drag and drop the move() steps block from Motion palette and put 4. This will make the Beetle move towards the mouse-pointer 4 steps.

  • Repeat the above 2 steps continuously throughout the project by adding a forever block from the Control palette.

  • The project is now complete. Click on the green flag and enjoy playing! Save your Taco from the Beetle.

Save the project file as Beetle and Taco.

outcomes: Understand the Jumping Movement in Pictobox
Understand Gliding Movement in Pictoblox
Understand moving with Steps in Pictoblox
Understand Skipping Movement in Pictoblox
introduction: As we have already learned how to use the two or more sprites in PictoBlox, now in this session, you will learn about controlling the sprites to develop a Game. The concept behind this session is to solidify knowledge about the Control palette and to know each block related to it. This game has two parts, in the first part is to learn about making a sprite (Monkey) to move with the keys on the keyboard. In the second part, you learn to control each sprite (Monkey and Apple) accordingly. Let’s make it!

notes.title: Ice Breaker
notes.content:
Two Truths and a Lie:

  • Get the class to tell two statements about themselves that are obviously true and one that is a lie

  • Other class members should try guess which the lie is

  • Have fun and be creative!

notes.title: Activity
notes.content: In this activity, we will learn how to make a sprite move with the keys of a keyboard? And create a game to catch the falling apple.

  • Ask participants to share their main takeaways or insights.

In this class activity,

Encourage active participation in dialogue and motion among the class participants.

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content:
Combining motion


In the last session, you learned how to make a sprite follow another sprite  to create more dynamic projects. Building on that concept, this session will introduce you to a different type of interaction: controlling sprites with keyboard input.

You'll apply this new skill by beginning to create the "Apple and Monkey" game. In this first part, you'll learn how to use events and triggers to make your Monkey sprite move left and right in response to key presses. This is a fundamental concept for creating any game where the player controls a character.

Let's start!

mainConcepts.title: Let’s Play
mainConcepts.content:
Who doesn’t like playing games!

Events:

Events blocks are related to various triggers in a project, or when one part signals another to run.

When()key pressed: Scripts placed underneath this block will activate when the specified key is pressed.

In this activity, we will learn how to make a sprite move with the keys of a keyboard? And create a game to catch the falling apple.

Let’s Code

Follow the steps below:

  1. Create a New file in PictoBlox. Open PictoBlox and create a new file. Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

  3. Click the Choose a Backdrop button. Select the Woods And Bench backdrop from the library.

  • Click the Choose a Sprite button. Select the Monkey and Apple sprite from the library.

  • Right-click on the Tobi icon in the sprite palette and delete it. Your stage is all set for coding.

  • Select the Monkey sprite by clicking its icon.

  • Open the Events palette and drag and drop the when flag clicked block into the scripting area.

  • Snap go to x () y () block from Motion palette, to make the initial position set at x to 0 and y to -100.

  • Snap set size to () block from Looks palette and set the size to 80%.

  • We will use a when () key pressed block from the Events palette to write a small script to make the Monkey sprite move left using the left arrow key. The ‘-‘ sign indicates that the sprite should move left.

  • Similarly, we will use another when () key pressed block to write a small script to make the Monkey sprite move right using the right arrow key.

Check the scripts till now:

Thats it for part 1. lets continue.

Let’s Play! – Part 2

Let’s continue the last project. Till now, we have made the controls for the monkey.

 

Sensing:

Sensing blocks are associate with sprites and stage detecting conditions.

Touching():The block checks if its sprite is touching the mouse-pointer, edge, or another sprite. If the sprite is touching the selected object, the block returns true; if it is not, it returns false.

Pick random() to (): The block picks a pseudorandom number ranging from the first given number to the second, including both endpoints. If both numbers have no decimals, it will report a whole number. For example, if a 1 and a 3 were inputted, the block could return a 1, 2 or 3. If one of the numbers has a decimal point, even .0, it reports a number with a decimal. For example, if 0.1 and 0.14 were given, the output will be 0.1, 0.11, 0.12, 0.13, or 0.14.

We will continue from the last project. Open the project file in PictoBlox.

Till now, we have made the controls for the monkey.

Making the Script for the Apple

  1. Select the Apple sprite by clicking its icon.

  3. Set its initial position to 0,140.

  5. Now, when an apple falls, it falls vertically, i.e. along the Y-axis. Therefore, to make make the Apple sprite fall, we must change only the value of the y coordinate by some amount, say -5.

  7. We will add a wait block to make it fall slowly.

  9. Since it should keep falling, we will add a forever block to make it continuous.

Script for Sensing the Monkey

Now we want to check if the Apple sprite touched the Monkey sprite. We will write this script while keeping the Apple sprite selected.

  1. To check if the Apple sprite touched the Monkey sprite, we will use the touching ()? block from the Sensing palette and drop it inside the space of an if () then block from the Control palette.

  3. Now, it touches the Monkey sprite, we want it to bounce off and glide to a random position. For that, we will use two pick random () to () blocks from the Operators palette for both the x and y coordinates and drop them inside the glide () secs to x: () y: () block.

Script for Sensing the Edge

  1. To sense whether Apple sprite reaches the lower edge of the stage or not, we will again use the touching () ? block and drop it inside the space of another if () then block.

  3. If the Apple sprite touches the edge, the game should stop. For that, we will use the stop(all) block from the Control palette to stop all the scripts.

And we are done. Maximize the stage and enjoy the game!

Save the project file as Apple and Monkey.

outcomes: Using buttons on Quarky
Moving a Sprite in the 4 directions-up
Moving a Sprite in the 4 directions - Up/left/down/right
Using buttons on Quarky to move a Sprite
introduction: In our previous sessions, you've learned to control sprites with code blocks, keyboard presses, and even with the mouse. This session marks an exciting new step as you're introduced to the world of robotics. By the end of this session, you'll be able to create a project where you can make Tobi move left and right simply by pressing the L and R buttons on the Quarky board. This is a crucial step toward building projects that start your journey in Robotics

notes.title: Ice Breaker
notes.content:
Draw My Life

You can have your kids draw themselves, their likes, their interests, their dreams, and a cool fact about them in a 4–5-page booklet. You can then have your students share their booklets with each other and talk about the different pages.

notes.title: Activity
notes.content: Tobi Control – In this activity, we are going to be controlling Tobi’s movements. We’re going to do it using Quarky’s buttons. The logic is simple – if we press the left switch, L, Tobi move left. If we press the right switch, R, he should move right.

notes.title: Wrap Activity
notes.content:
1. Summarize the entire lesson.Ask the students:

    1. What we have learned in the session?

    2. What are x and y axis?

    3. Discuss on the switches on Quarky?

  2. Seek the class for doubts regarding the topic.

Things to Remember Before Class

Make sure each PC has the latest version of PictoBlox installed and running. Make sure the following required materials are available;

  • Quarky Board

  • USB Cable (C-type)

  • Make sure that Quarky’s battery is charged and working properly.

  • Make sure that Quarky is connected to the computer.

During Class

If the students are unable to find a block, point them to the color of the block on the block palette. Make sure they write the script by selecting the appropriate sprite. Make sure that they have connected Quarky correctly.

After Class

Make sure students save their codes/scripts.

Make sure they have disconnected Quarky and powered off.

Make sure they shut down the PC/Laptops.

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content: Make Tobi Move – Overview

mainConcepts.title: Drawing a square
mainConcepts.content:
Drawing a square

Make Tobi Move – Overview

Before we start making our game, we need to understand one very important part of the game – controlling Tobi’s movements. We’re going to do it using Quarky’s buttons.

The logic is simple – if we press the left switch, L, Tobi to move left. If we press the right switch, R, he should move right.

Quarky Buttons

Quarky has 2 tactile switches or buttons – L and R, which allow Quarky to take input from the user. You can get the state of the button in PictoBlox using is button () pressed? block. The block reports 1 when the switch is pressed and 0 when the switch is not pressed.

Let’s Code!

Follow the steps below:

Let’s begin by first connecting Quarky to PictoBlox:

First, connect Quarky to your laptop using the USB cable.

Open PictoBlox and create a new file. Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

Select the Board as Quarky.

Next, select the Serial port to connect Quarky and press Connect.

Go to the Sensors palette and add a is button () pressed ? block into the scripting area. The button, L, is set as the default option.To check if L is pressed, we’ll use an if block.

Go to the Control palette and drop an if block into the scripting area. Drop the is button () pressed ? block in the white space of the if block.If L is pressed, then, according to our logic, Tobi should move left.

Therefore, go to the Motion palette and drop a move () steps block inside the if block and write -10 in the space. The “-” sign means that direction of movement is left.

However, if R is pressed, then Tobi should move right. Therefore, add another if block below the first one and drop a is button () pressed ? block in the white space.Th

en, go to the Motion palette and drop a move () steps block inside the if block and write 10 in the space.

Add a forever block around the entire script.Add a when flag clicked block from the Events palette above the forever block to add the finishing touch.

Finally, click the green flag to run the script.

﻿Save the project file as Tobi Control.

outcomes: Creating speech in Pictoblox
Combine Movement and Dialogue/Speech
introduction:
In this lesson you will learn how to make our character talk while in different motions.

  • Create a script that takes input from the user and chats with them.

  • Explain various parts of the Graphical Programming Interface i.e. Stage, Sprite, and Script.

.

notes.content:
This or That

This is one of the most effective ice-breakers for kids, as it involves simply going from one side of the room to another. You can start by saying, “Dog lovers on the left, cat lovers on the right.” You can have them talk about why they like or dislike certain things and open up a dialogue.

Before Class

  2. Make sure the following required materials are available

  3. Make sure that Quarky’s battery is charged and working properly.

  4. Make sure that Quarky is connected to the computer.

During Class

  2. Make sure they write the script by selecting the appropriate sprite.

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content:
In the last sessions, you were introduced to the PictoBlox interface, costumes, motion and sound

  • This session will now introduce you to a new concept: interaction. You will learn how to create a program that can communicate with the user by asking questions and responding to their answers. By the end of this session, you will be able to create a chat-based project where a sprite can talk to you and your friends, making your animations more engaging and personal.

mainConcepts.title: Chatty sprite
mainConcepts.content:
Chatty sprite

What if your sprite could talk to you or your friend? Let’s find out how!

Asking a question

To make the sprite ask a question, we will use an ask block from the Sensing palette. It will ask the user whatever you write inside it, wait for user input, and then store that input in the answer block.

Switching costume

Costume: A costume is one out of possibly many “frames” or alternate appearances of a sprite. Sprites can change their look to any of their costumes.

Every sprite has at least one costume.

One of the most common uses of costumes is to make an animation for a game. You can use a number of costumes to create one single animation. The costumes available for the sprite are shown in the Costume tab, next to the python tab on the extreme left.

In this activity, we will learn how to ask a user to enter input and create a script where a friendly goblin has a small chat with the user.

Let’s Code

Follow the steps below:

  1. Open PictoBlox and create a new file. Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

  3. Click the Choose a Backdrop button. Select the Castle 2 backdrop from the library.

  • Click the Choose a Sprite button and Select the Goblin sprite from the library.

Right-click on the Tobi icon in the sprite palette and delete it. Your stage is all set for coding.

Create the script to make the Goblin switch costume to goblin-a & go to 130, 70 on the stage when the green flag is clicked.

Add glide () secs to x () y () block from Motion palette. This will make Goblin glide to the front of the stage. Make x as -100 and y as -40.

Next, we will use the switch costume to () and say () for () seconds block from the Looks palette to make the Goblin look like he’s talking.

To make the Goblin ask a question, we will use an ask and wait block from the Sensing It will ask the user whatever you write inside it, wait for user input, and then store that input in the answer block.

First, we will use the answer block from the Sensing palette to read the user input.

To combine our message with the user’s answer, we will use a join block from the Operator palette.

Finally, to reply, we will use another say () for () seconds block from the Looks palette.

Place a say block below the ask and wait block and then place a join block into the first space given.

Write “Nice to meet you ” in the first space of the join block and place the answer block into the second space of the join block.

The complete script looks like this:

Maximize the stage and enjoy the animation!

Save the project file as Let Us Chat!

Activities

outcomes: Understand the importance of Wait Blocks and how to use them.
Broadcasting
The plan and design of Story-telling
Dialogue
introduction: In this lesson, you will explore the art of storytelling through coding by using PictoBlox. You'll learn how to make two sprites communicate with each other using event blocks, which trigger actions and enable interaction between different parts of your project. By the end of the session, you will create an engaging animation where a Fox and a Lion discuss the challenges they face in the forest. This hands-on activity will not only introduce you to the fundamentals of event-based programming but also spark your creativity in designing interactive stories.

notes.title: Ice Breaker
notes.content:
Two truths one lie

Each participant shares two true statements and one false statement about themselves.

The rest of the group tries to guess which statement is a lie.

This activity helps participants get to know each other in a fun and interactive way.

notes.title: Activity
notes.content: Story Making – We will learn how to make two sprites communicate and then use the concept to tell a story of a fox and lion in which they talk about the problems they are facing in the forest.

notes.title: Wrap Activity
notes.content:
• Wrap up the class by summarizing the key concepts covered and highlighting their importance in programming.

  • Discuss the topics that will be covered in the next class, creating anticipation and motivation for continued learning.

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content:
In the last session, you learned how to create an animation with an interactive character that could ask you questions and respond to your answers.

Building on that, this session will introduce you to a new level of animation: sprite communication.

You'll learn how to make two or more sprites communicate with each other using the broadcast block. You'll begin creating a short story with a fox and a lion, where the fox will "speak" and then signal the lion to begin its part of the conversation. This fundamental concept is crucial for creating more complex narratives and dynamic interactions between characters in your projects.

mainConcepts.title: Chatty sprite
mainConcepts.content:
Chatty sprite

Did you know that you could also tell a story in PictoBlox? Here’s how!

Events:

Events blocks are related to various triggers in a project, or when one-part signals another to run.

Broadcast(): The block sends a broadcast throughout the whole Scratch program. Any scripts in any sprites that are hatted with the When I Receive () block that is set to a specified broadcast will activate. This broadcast block lets scripts send broadcasts without any waits in its script (unlike the Broadcast () and Wait block). Broadcasts are a good way to have sprites and scripts communicate.

Broadcast() and wait: The block sends a broadcast throughout the whole Scratch project — any scripts that are halted with the When I Receive () block and are set to the broadcast will activate. This broadcast block lets scripts send broadcasts, and have them wait until all scripts are activated by the broadcast ends.

When I receive(): Scripts that begin with this block will be invoked once the specified broadcast has been sent by a calling script. If a Forever block is sending the broadcast faster than the attached script can finish running, the script will restart without reaching the blocks at the end. This can be avoided by using broadcast () and wait.

In this activity, We learn how to make two sprites communicate and then use the concept to tell a story of a fox and lion in which they talk about the problems they are facing in the forest.

Let’s get started!

Let’s Code

Follow the steps below:

  1. Create a New file in PictoBlox. Open PictoBlox and create a new file. Select the coding environment as Block Coding.

  1. Click the Choose a Backdrop button. Select the Savanna backdrop from the library.

Click the Choose a Sprite button. Select the Fox and Lion sprites from the library.

  • Right-click on the Tobi icon in the sprite palette and delete it. Your stage is all set for coding.

Making the First Script for the Fox

  1. Select the Fox sprite by clicking its icon.

  1. Using the go to x:() y:() block, we will set the initial position of the Fox sprite.

  1. Then using the say () for () seconds block, we will make the Fox sprite initiate a conversation with the Lion sprite.

  1. Then we will use a broadcast block from the Events palette. It will broadcast a message to the Lion sprite so that the other sprite can act according to the script.

Making the Script for the Lion

  1. Select the Lion sprite by clicking its icon and set its initial position using another go to x:() y:() block.

  1. Flip the sprite horizontally by using the Flip Horizontal command in the edit costume section.

Receiving the Message and Replying to the Fox

  1. To make the Lion sprite sense the message broadcast by the Fox sprite, we will use a when I receive hat block from the Events palette.

  1. Then, we will make the Lion sprite answer the Fox sprite’s question using say () for () seconds blocks and use another broadcast block to broadcast message2 for the Fox sprite.

Save the project as once upon a time part 1

Click the green flag and enjoy the animation!

outcomes: Clearly differentiate the x and y-axis and make the left and right movements using the keyboard.
Attain knowledge of the PictoBlox application and its interfaces.
Create a game where a Monkey catches the falling apple.
introduction: As we have already learned how to use the two or more sprites in PictoBlox, now in this session you are going to learning about controlling the sprites to develop a Game. The concept behind this session is to solidify knowledge about the Control palette and to know each block related to it. This game has two parts, in the first part students are learning about making a sprite (Monkey) to move with the keys on the keyboard. In the second part, students are learning to control each sprite (Monkey and Apple) accordingly. Let’s make it!

notes.title: Ice Breaker
notes.content:
Quizziz questions recap
Issue a quiz and revise on the questions set

notes.title: Activity
notes.content: Story Making – We will learn how to make two sprites communicate and then use the concept to tell a story of a fox and lion in which they talk about the problems they are facing in the forest.

notes.title: Wrap Activity
notes.content:
• Summarize the key points covered in the session.

  • Ask participants to share their main takeaways or insights.

  • Provide additional resources, such as recommended books, websites, or online tutorials, for participants to explore further.

Encourage participation among the class paticipants

Encourage team work

mainConcepts.title: Introduction
mainConcepts.content:
In the last session, you started a story where a fox began a conversation and a lion responded.

This session will build on that dialogue to make the conversation longer and more complex. You'll learn how to complete the back-and-forth by making the fox receive the lion's reply and then deliver its own final lines.

This process will reinforce your understanding of using the When I receive () and broadcast () block to manage communication between multiple sprites, allowing you to create more detailed and engaging stories.

mainConcepts.title: Story Making – Once Upon a Time PART II
mainConcepts.content:
Story Making – Once Upon a Time PART II

We have already created such an animation! we will be making the conversation longer! Lets dive into it!

Open your saved file once upon a time part 1

Making the Second Script for the Fox

First, we will select the Fox sprite by clicking its icon.Then, just like we did for the Lion sprite, we will make the Fox sprite sense the message broadcast by the Lion sprite using the when I receive hat block and select message2 in the drop-down.Then, we will make the Fox sprite reply to the Lion sprite using say () for () seconds blocks.

Maximize the stage and enjoy the animation!

﻿

You can add these concepts to your current animation:

Sounds: Use blocks from the Sound palette to add sound effects or music to your animation.

Movement: Apply Motion blocks to make your sprites move, glide, or turn as part of the story. You can also use key presses or mouse clicks to control the movement.

Costumes and Backdrops: Use switch costume to and switch backdrop to blocks to change your sprites' appearance or the scene.

This will make our animation more engaging and realistic just like the animations we watch on tv!

﻿Save the project file as Once upon a time 2.

outcomes: Complete the game where a Monkey catches the falling apple.
Code a meaningful project
introduction: In this session, the focus is on learning how to control individual sprites effectively by using the Control palette in your programming environment. Building on what you learned in the previous lesson about moving the Monkey sprite using keyboard inputs, this lesson aims to deepen your understanding of how each block in the Control palette works. You’ll explore the purpose and function of these blocks, gaining the skills to apply them creatively to control various sprites. This step will help you solidify your knowledge and enhance your ability to create more interactive and dynamic projects. Let’s dive in and bring your sprites to life with better control!

notes.title: Ice Breaker
notes.content:
Try Not to Laugh!

You can have your students sit in a circle for this activity and have each of them try to make the other one laugh. The other kids must stay silent as students use props, funny faces, silly dances, and goofy gestures to get them to laugh.

notes.title: Activity
notes.content: In this activity, you will be using the knowledge of story making to create a simple animation.

notes.title: Wrap Activity
notes.content:
Wrap Activity

  1. Summarize the entire lesson one by one.Ask the students:

    1. What we have learned in the session?

    2. Different blocks that we used in this activity.

  2. Seek the class for doubts regarding the topic.

Learning Outcome

By implementing these scripts, learners will:

  • Understand how to structure a sprite's movement.

  • Learn to sense and react to interactions between sprites or the game environment.

  • Gain confidence in using conditional statements, loops, and operators to build dynamic and interactive projects.

  • These foundational skills will prepare learners to create more complex behaviors and interactions in their Scratch or similar programming projects.

mainConcepts.content: Dialogue

mainConcepts.content:
Dialogue

Have 3 or more sprites.

Have some sounds in the project.

Have movement in the project.

Have hide and show in the animation.

Be creative.

Save the project as my animation 1.

import mysql.connector
# Connect to MySQL
db = mysql.connector.connect(
    host="localhost",
    user="root",
    password="your_password",
    database="digifunzi_lms"
)
cursor = db.cursor()
# Execute query
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM courses")
# Fetch results
results = cursor.fetchall()
# Print results
for row in results:
    print(row)
# Close connection
cursor.close()
db.close()
